segments.s051-g17: کمین‌گاه را جای شایسته دید uses the construction X را Y دید, where را marks the definite object (کمین‌گاه) and جای شایسته is a predicate nominal: literally 'he saw the ambush-site (as) a fitting place.' دید is the past tense verb 'saw/considered' and here functions like 'considered.' Word order (object + predicate + past verb) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s051-g18: سواران جنگی و بایسته دید continues the same verb (دید) with an omitted را; سواران is the noun being judged and جنگی و بایسته are coordinated adjectives/predicate complements ('warlike and fitting'). The verb دید again carries the meaning 'he considered/held (them) to be...'; omission of را is normal in poetic compression.
segments.s051-g19: چو شب تیره شد introduces a temporal clause: چو = 'when', شب تیره شد = 'night became dark' (تیره شد = past 'became dark'). The clause then presents the subject 'تور' followed by the accompaniment phrase 'با صدهزار' ('with one hundred thousand'). In classical Persian, the temporal clause precedes the main action.
segments.s051-g20: بیامد کمربسته کارزار is the main clause: بیامد (past 'came') plus an adjectival/participial phrase کمربسته کارزار meaning 'girded/ready for battle.' Here کمربسته functions as a participial adjective modifying the coming action (he came 'belted'/'ready'), and کارزار specifies the purpose/field (battle).
segments.s051-g21: شبیخون سگالیده و ساخته is a noun (شبیخون, 'night-raid') followed by two past-participial adjectives joined by و: سگالیده and ساخته. Both adjectives modify شبیخون ('set-upon/assailed' or 'contrived' and 'made/prepared'), forming a compressed descriptive phrase — a typical poetic stacking of modifiers after the noun.
segments.s051-g22: بپیوسته تیر و کمان آخته: بپیوسته is an adverb meaning 'constantly/continuously' placed before the coordinated noun phrase تیر و کمان, and آخته is a participial adjective ('ready/drawn') that post-modifies that phrase. The whole unit means 'with arrow and bow continuously ready' (i.e. bows strung and arrows at hand).
segments.s051-g23: چو آمد سپه دید بر جای خویش is a temporal clause plus main observation: چو آمد ('when [it] came') with سپه (the host/army) as subject; دید is past 'he saw/found.' بر جای خویش is a prepositional phrase 'in/on their own place' where خویش is the reflexive possessive ('their own'); note خوی + ش is split in the text but reads together as 'their own.'
segments.s051-g24: درفش فروزنده بر پای پیش is a noun phrase giving the salient detail: درفش (banner) modified by the active/participial adjective فروزنده ('radiant/illuminating'). بر پای پیش is a prepositional locution literally 'on the front foot' i.e. 'at the foremost position'; together: 'the radiant standard at the front.'
segments.s051-g1: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). The phrase follows a typical Persian order with the time expression before the verb: 'از روز رخشنده نیمی' = 'half of the bright day' (note the adjective رخشنده modifies روز). برفت is the clause-final verbal element indicating that that half of the day 'went by / had passed'; this is a poetic/past narrative form.
segments.s051-g2: دل functions as the head noun 'the heart' and is followed without the expected ezāfe by the phrase هر دو جنگی (poetic omission) meaning 'of both warriors/warriors both'. ز introduces cause/agent 'from/because of' and کینه is the noun 'hatred, grudge'. بتفت is a poetic verbal form meaning 'burned/scorched' or 'was consumed' — here predicated of the hearts, i.e. 'their hearts burned with hatred'.
segments.s051-g3: به تدبیر is an instrumental/intent phrase 'by/with a plan' (به + noun). یک با دگر literally 'one with another' = 'one after another' or 'with one another' (an adverbial phrase). ساختند is the clause-final past tense plural verb 'they made/devised', so the whole segment means 'they devised one plan after another'.
segments.s051-g4: همه رای is the direct object noun phrase 'all counsel(s)/every plan'; بیهوده is an adverb/adjective 'in vain / useless'. انداختند is the past tense plural verb 'they threw/cast' used idiomatically here to mean 'they dismissed/threw away' (i.e. treated counsel as worthless). The verb is clause-final as is normal in Persian.
segments.s051-g5: که here introduces the content of their plan ('that / such that'). چون شب شود is a temporal clause 'when night becomes (comes)'; چون functions as a temporal conjunction. ما شبیخون کنیم contains the subject ما 'we' and the verb کنیم in the subjunctive/volitional sense 'we will make/do' (classical Persian uses the present-subjunctive for intended future actions), with شبیخون ('night-raid') as the object.
segments.s051-g6: همه دشت و هامون is a coordinated noun phrase 'all the plains and wastes' (همه here = 'all/the whole'). پر از خون is a predicative phrase 'full of blood' (پر + از + noun). The clause ends with کنیم (same volitional/subjunctive verb), repeating the intended action: 'we will fill ... with blood.'
segments.s051-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کارآگهان is a plural noun/epithet 'the knowledgeable/informed ones' (those aware of affairs). آگهی یافتند is a compound verbal phrase: آگهی 'news/information' as object and یافتند 'they received/found' as the past-tense verb, so together 'they received the news'.
segments.s051-g8: دوان is an adverbial/participle meaning 'running/rushed'; it precedes the prepositional/directional phrase introducing the destination. زی is an archaic preposition here meaning 'to/unto' (classical Persian usage) and introduces the proper name منوچهر, the destination. بشتافتند is the past plural verb 'they hurried/ran toward' placed at the end of the segment.
segments.s051-g9: رسیدند is a past plural verb 'they arrived/came'. پیش + noun functions as 'before/in the presence of' and follows the verb phrase here, so the sequence means 'they came before Manuchehr the king' (subject implied elsewhere). منظًور: word order is Verb + prepositional phrase indicating the addressee.
segments.s051-g10: بگفتند is past plural 'they said'. تا here is a purposive marker 'so that / in order that' connecting the reported speech to a purpose clause. برنشاند is a classical/periphrastic past-inflected form meaning 'to seat / place (someone)' (here 'seat/deploy the army'). سپاه is the direct object 'army, host' of that verb; the clause reports the speakers' request.
segments.s051-g11: منوچهر is the subject. بشنید is a simple past 'he heard'. و links two coordinated predicates; بگشاد is a past form 'opened' (here with the old verbal prefix ب-), and گوش is the object of بگشاد — idiomatically 'to open one's ear' = 'to listen attentively'.
segments.s051-g12: This phrase has an intransitive result clause: سوی 'toward' + چاره 'remedy/solution' + شد 'became/turned' — 'turned toward a remedy'. مرد بسیار هوش is an appositive/epithet describing the subject ('a very intelligent man'); شد here is simple past 'became/turned' linking the action to that described person.
segments.s051-g13: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as definite object. سراسر is an adverb/adjectival particle meaning 'throughout/entire(ly)' modifying the transfer. به + proper/name قارن indicates the recipient ('to Qaren'). سپرد is past 'entrusted/handed over' — overall: 'he entrusted the whole army to Qaren.'
segments.s051-g14: بگزید is past 'chose'. In classical Persian poetic word order the subject may follow the verb, so 'کمین‌گاه بگزید سالار گرد' reads as 'the (chief) commander chose the ambush-site.' سالار گرد functions as the title/subject 'the commander (of the host)'; here گرد is part of that classical epithet. The clause is a simple past nominal-verb sequence with inverted subject.
segments.s051-g15: ببرد here is a classical past/preterite form meaning 'he took/seized' (the b- prefix appears in older narrative forms). از marks the source 'from', سران 'the chiefs/leaders', نامور 'renowned', and سی‌هزار is the numeral 'thirty-thousand' — together: 'he took thirty thousand from the famed chiefs.'
segments.s051-g16: This is a coordinated noun phrase giving what was taken: دلیران 'the brave ones' (plural noun), و 'and' linking, گردان 'troops/troop-units', modified by the compound خنجر‌گزار 'knife/dagger-bearers' (an adjective or agent-noun). The phrase enumerates the kinds of men/sectors taken.
segments.s051-g33: برآورد here is a past action meaning 'he lifted/raised' (an archaic/poetic use of برآوردن). شاه is the subject (the king). از کمین‌گاه is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the ambush' following the verb, and سر ('head') is the thing lifted — poetic word order places the object-like noun after the locative phrase.
segments.s051-g34: نبد is a negative/existential past 'there was not' (or 'there did not exist'). تور را uses the object marker را to mark 'the net' as definite. از دو رویه گذر is a compound meaning 'a passage from/toward the two sides' (دو رویه = both sides/faces, گذر = passage), so the clause says no passage existed through the net on either side.
segments.s051-g35: عنان را بپیچید is SOV: انان (the reins) marked with را as definite object and بپیچید is a past-tense verb 'he twisted/turned (the reins)'. و links the clauses; برگاشت is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning 'he turned/turned back' and روی is its object 'face' (i.e. 'he turned his face').
segments.s051-g36: برآمد is a past intransitive 'arose/issued forth'. ز لشکر is the source 'from the army/host'. یکی‌های is an archaic plural/indefinite marker meaning 'one(s)/someone(s)' or 'a certain (group of)'; هوی here is an interjection/cry (battle‑cry 'Hū!'), so the whole clause says that from the host a cry of 'Hū' arose.
segments.s051-g37: دمان literally 'skirt/hem' is used metonymically for 'the rear/retinue' or 'from the rear'; از پس = 'from/after/behind'; ایدر is a poetic 'here'; the proper name منوچهر followed by شاه identifies the arriving subject 'Manuchehr the king'. Word order places the locative/partitive phrase before the name for emphasis.
segments.s051-g38: رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached'. اندر آن = 'into/among that' or 'there' (poetic positioning). نامور is an epithet 'renowned/famous' modifying the following compound کینه خواه: کینه (grudge/hatred) + خواه (one who desires) forms the compound noun/adjective 'seeker of vengeance' or 'vengeful one'.
segments.s051-g39: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'. نیزه is the accusative object 'a spear' and انداخت is the past verb 'threw/hurled'. بر پشت او is a prepositional phrase 'upon/at his back' specifying the target.
segments.s051-g40: This clause inverts normal order for poetic effect: نگونسار شد is 'became woeful/turned mournful' (شد = became). خنجر از مشت او is a noun phrase 'a dagger from his fist' (i.e. a dagger sprang from his hand). Together the sense is that a woeful/dreadful dagger issued from his fist.
segments.s051-g41: ز (preposition) = 'from' (poetic/zast form) + زین 'saddle' (noun). برگرفتش is برگرفت (past simple 'took/ seized') with the enclitic ـش = 'him/it', so literally 'he took him from the saddle'. به کردار باد is an adverbial phrase: به + کردار ('in the manner/way') + باد ('wind'), i.e. 'in the manner of the wind' = 'swiftly, like the wind'. Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase (from the saddle) + verb+enclitic, then an adjunct of manner introduced by به.
segments.s051-g42: بزد is the past tense (simple past) of زدن 'to strike/hit' — 'he struck'. بر زمین is the locative phrase 'onto the ground' (بر = on/upon, زمین = ground). The remainder 'داد مردی بداد' is idiomatic/poetic: داد here is the verb 'gave', مردی is the indefinite/direct-object 'a man' (or 'the man'), and بداد is an archaic/poetic noun meaning 'a bad fate/oppression' used here figuratively to indicate a violent, fatal outcome. The clause thus strings two past actions: he struck him to the ground, and (literally) 'gave the man a bad fate' → idiomatically 'dealt him a fatal blow'.
segments.s051-g43: سرش را: سر 'head' + ـش enclitic 'his' + را (definite object marker) — 'his head' as the definite object. هم آنگه is a temporal/discourse sequence: هم 'also/even' + آنگه 'then', giving 'then also/after that'. ز تن دور کرد is z + تن + دور + کرد: ز 'from', تن 'body', دور 'away/removed' (here adjectival/adverbial meaning 'separate/away'), کرد (past of کردن 'to do/make') — 'he made (it) distant from the body' i.e. 'he separated/cut it off'. The verb کردن carries the causative/resultative sense 'made/separated'.
segments.s051-g44: This clause is compact/poetic: دد و دام را is a coordinated noun phrase (دد 'enemy/hostility' and دام 'trap') with را marking it as object. از تنش 'from his body' (از = from; تن + ـش = his body). سور کرد is a verbal phrase where سور is a noun 'feast/banquet' used with کرد 'made' — literally 'made a feast' or figuratively 'made (a display/celebration) of'. In context the expression conveys that he treated the fallen man's body as a spoil/spectacle (the phrase is idiomatic/poetic). Grammatically, the verb کردن makes سور into a verbal complement: 'made (it) a feast' / 'made a feast of (it)'.
segments.s051-g45: بیامد is the past tense 'he came/returned'. به + لشکرگه is the prepositional phrase 'to the army-camp' (لشکرگه = camp). خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'his/own' modifying لشکرگه; the enclitic ـش signals third-person possession. باز 'again' is adverbial and here follows the noun phrase for emphasis: literal order 'came to his camp again'. Word order (verb + prepositional phrase + possessor + adverb) is acceptable in poetic Persian.
segments.s051-g46: به دیدار is the conventional Persian phrase 'to the sight/meeting of' meaning 'to visit' or 'to see'. آن 'that/the' indexes the following noun لشکر 'army/host', and سرفراز is an adjective 'victorious/proud' modifying لشکر. Grammatically this is a typical prepositional phrase of purpose/direction: 'to (the) meeting/seeing of that victorious army' = 'to see that victorious army'.
segments.s051-g25: The phrase 'جز از ... چاره ندید' is a classical Persian clause meaning 'he saw no solution except ...'. 'جز' is the exception preposition and is followed by 'از' here (poetic 'except from'). 'چاره' is the object 'remedy/solution' and 'ندید' is the past negative of 'دیدن' (did not see), placed at the end as is normal in Persian.
segments.s051-g26: This clause means 'a cry rose from among the army.' 'خروش' is the subject 'a shout/roar', 'از میان سپه' is a source phrase 'from the midst of the host', and 'بر کشید' (here written as two words) is the verbal phrase 'rose up / was raised' — 'بر' functions as a verbal adverbial particle 'up/forth' and 'کشید' is the past tense of 'کشیدن' used here in the sense 'to raise/raise up'.
segments.s051-g27: The prepositional phrase 'ز گرد سواران' ('from around the horsemen') precedes the rest. 'هوا' ('the air') is the object, and 'بست' is a past verb meaning 'closed/struck/filled' (here poetically 'cleft/filled the air' by the blade). 'میغ' (blade) comes at the end as the agent — classical word order often places the verb before the agent noun.
segments.s051-g28: 'چو' introduces a simile 'like/as'. 'برق درخشنده' is a descriptive phrase 'shining lightning', and 'پولاد تیغ' is a compound noun 'steel sword' with the material 'پولاد' qualifying 'تیغ'. The whole phrase modifies the preceding action: the sword gleamed like lightning.
segments.s051-g29: 'هوا را' is the definite object/topic ('the air'). 'تو گفتی' is the addressed 'you said' (classical second-person past). The particle 'همی' marks continued or emphatic aspect before the verb, and 'برفروخت' is a past tense verb meaning 'was kindled / gave off flame' — together: 'you said the air was kindled.'
segments.s051-g30: 'چو' again introduces a simile 'as/like'. 'الماس' is the image 'diamond', 'روی زمین' ('the surface/face of the earth') is the object marked by 'را', and 'بسوخت' is the past intransitive/impersonal 'was burned' or 'burned' — so: 'it burned the face/surface of the earth like a diamond.' The object precedes the verb, typical of Persian poetic order.
segments.s051-g31: 'به مغز اندرون' is a locative/directional phrase 'into the innermost core/heart'. 'بانگ پولاد' is a compound 'the cry/clang of steel' (بانگ modified by پولاد), and 'خاست' is the archaic past 'arose' (from خاستن). The clause literally reads 'into the inner core the cry of steel arose.'
segments.s051-g32: 'به ابر اندرون' ('into the cloud's interior') is another locative phrase. 'آتش و باد' ('fire and wind') are coordinated subjects and 'خاست' (again the past 'arose') follows; classical Persian frequently uses a singular past verb after coordinated nouns. The sense is 'fire and wind rose up inside the cloud.'

sentences.s051-s9: He judged the ambush-site a fitting place, and the horsemen warlike and suitable.
sentences.s051-s10: When night grew dark, Tur came with a hundred thousand, girded for battle.
sentences.s051-s11: They prepared a contrived night-raid and kept bow and arrow constantly ready.
sentences.s051-s12: When the host arrived, he saw them in their places, the radiant standard at the front.
sentences.s051-s1: When half of the bright day had passed, the hearts of both warriors burned with hatred.
sentences.s051-s2: They contrived one plan after another; they cast aside every counsel as useless.
sentences.s051-s3: When night comes, we will make a night-raid; we will fill every plain and waste with blood.
sentences.s051-s4: When the informed ones received the news, they hurried to Manuchehr.
sentences.s051-s5: They came before King Manuchehr and said, so that he might seat/deploy the army.
sentences.s051-s6: Manuchehr heard and opened his ear; the very wise man turned toward a remedy.
sentences.s051-s7: He entrusted the entire army to Qaren; the commander chose the ambush-site.
sentences.s051-s8: He took (seized) thirty thousand from the famed chiefs — brave men and battalions of dagger-bearers.
sentences.s051-s17: The king lifted his head out of the ambush; there was no passage through the net on either side.
sentences.s051-s18: He twisted the reins and turned his face; from the host there rose a shout of 'Hū!'
sentences.s051-s19: From here behind came Manuchehr the king — that renowned seeker of vengeance.
sentences.s051-s20: Someone hurled a spear at his back; a woeful dagger sprang from his fist.
sentences.s051-s21: He snatched him from the saddle like the wind, struck him to the ground, and gave the man a fatal blow.
sentences.s051-s22: Then he also cut off his head from his body, and stripped from his body the enemy's garb and trap.
sentences.s051-s23: He returned again to his own camp to see that victorious army.
sentences.s051-s13: He saw no remedy except war and battle; a cry rose from the midst of the host.
sentences.s051-s14: From around the riders the blade cleft the air; like lightning the steel sword gleamed.
sentences.s051-s15: You said the air was kindled; like a diamond, it burned the surface of the earth.
sentences.s051-s16: A clang of steel rose into the innermost core; into the cloud's interior fire and wind arose.
